steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'Introductus est' translates to?

hints.to lead out: 'To lead out' is 'educere' — 'introductus est' comes from 'introducere' (to bring in)
hints.to instruct: 'To instruct' is 'instruere' — 'introducere' means to lead/bring in
hints.to build: 'To build' is 'construere' — 'introducere' means to bring in

steps.prompt: What tense and mood is 'Introductus est'?
steps.reveal: Introductus est = he was brought in

hints.present passive indicative: Present passive would be 'introducitur' — participle + 'est' is perfect passive
hints.pluperfect passive indicative: Pluperfect passive would be 'introductus erat' — 'est' makes it perfect
hints.perfect active indicative: 'Introductus est' is passive (participle + sum) — active perfect would be 'introduxit'

hints.duco, ducere, duxi, ductum: These are the parts of 'ducere' (to lead) — the base verb without the intro- prefix
hints.instruo, instruere, instruxi, instructum: These are the parts of 'instruere' (to instruct/build up)
hints.induco, inducere, induxi, inductum: These are the parts of 'inducere' (to lead in/induce) — close but different prefix

steps.reveal: Volturcius = Volturcius (nom., subject of passive verb)

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'Volturchum' — 'Volturcius' is nominative, subject of the passive verb
hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'Volturci' — this is nominative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'Volturci' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'Volturci' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'Volturci' ends in -i

hints.accusative: 'Sine' always takes the ablative, never the accusative
hints.genitive: 'Sine' governs the ablative — genitive is not used with 'sine'
hints.dative: 'Sine' governs the ablative — dative is not used with 'sine'

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'Galli' — 'Gallis' with -is is dative or ablative plural
hints.accusative: Accusative plural would be 'Gallos' — 'Gallis' is ablative (governed by 'sine')
hints.dative: While 'Gallis' could be dative plural, here 'sine' requires the ablative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'Galli' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'Galli' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'Galli' ends in -i

steps.prompt: Who are the 'Galli' in the context of the Catilinarian conspiracy?

hints.Gallic soldiers fighting for Rome: The Galli here are Allobrogian ambassadors whom the conspirators tried to recruit
hints.priests of Cybele: The 'Galli' priests of Cybele are a different use — here they are Gallic ambassadors
hints.Celtic mercenaries hired by Catiline: They were ambassadors of the Allobroges, not mercenaries — Cicero used them to trap the conspirators

steps.prompt: What is the significance of 'sine Gallis' for the legal proceeding?
steps.target: Volturcius was questioned separately to get independent testimony
choices: Volturcius was questioned separately to get independent testimony
the Gauls had already been executed
Volturcius was not a Gaul himself

hints.the Gauls had already been executed: The Gauls were very much alive — they were key witnesses brought in after Volturcius
hints.the Gauls refused to testify: The Gauls did testify — they were questioned separately to ensure independent accounts
hints.Volturcius was not a Gaul himself: While true, the point is procedural: separate questioning prevents coordinated testimony

translation: Volturcius was brought in without the Gauls
